---
title: Benchmark Document
subtitle: A Comprehensive Stress Test
author: calepin
date: "2025-06-01"
abstract: A heavyweight document for profiling calepin rendering performance. No executed code --- pure markup, cross-references, tabsets, footnotes, callouts, theorems, citations, raw blocks, spans, content visibility, and lots of prose and mathematics.
bibliography: library.bib
number-sections: true
keywords:
  - benchmarking
  - performance
  - rendering
---

# Introduction {#sec-intro}

@sec-theory provides the theoretical framework for our analysis, while @sec-litreview surveys the existing literature. The methods are detailed in @sec-methods, with results presented in @sec-results. We discuss the implications in @sec-discussion and extend the analysis in @sec-extended. The mathematical supplement in @sec-math provides additional theoretical insights, and the code appendix in @sec-appendix-code ensures reproducibility. The document title is "{{ meta.title }}" and the author is {{ meta.author }}.

[^1]: First footnote --- Ut enim ad minim veniam, quis nostrud exercitation.
[^2]: Second footnote --- Duis aute irure dolor in reprehenderit in voluptate.
[^3]: Third footnote --- Excepteur sint occaecat cupidatat non proident.


# Theoretical Framework {#sec-theory}

As noted in @sec-intro, the theoretical foundations rest on several key results. We begin with the Pythagorean theorem and build toward more advanced constructs. The classical references include @knuth1997art, @euler1748introductio, and @gauss1801disquisitiones.

## Core Theorems

::: {.theorem #thm-pythagoras}
In a right triangle with legs $a$ and $b$ and hypotenuse $c$, we have $a^2 + b^2 = c^2$.
:::

::: {.proof}
Construct squares on each side of the right triangle. The area of the square on the hypotenuse equals the sum of the areas of the squares on the other two sides. This can be shown by rearranging four copies of the original triangle within a square of side length $a + b$, yielding $c^2 = a^2 + b^2$.
:::

::: {.theorem #thm-cauchy-schwarz}
For vectors $\mathbf{u}$ and $\mathbf{v}$ in an inner product space,
$$
|\langle \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v} \rangle|^2 \leq \langle \mathbf{u}, \mathbf{u} \rangle \cdot \langle \mathbf{v}, \mathbf{v} \rangle.
$$
:::

::: {.proof}
Consider $\mathbf{w} = \mathbf{u} - t\mathbf{v}$ for $t \in \mathbb{R}$. Then $\langle \mathbf{w}, \mathbf{w} \rangle \geq 0$ yields a quadratic in $t$ whose discriminant must be non-positive.
:::

::: {.lemma #lem-triangle}
For any real numbers $x$ and $y$, $|x + y| \leq |x| + |y|$.
:::

::: {.corollary #cor-norm}
If $\|\cdot\|$ is a norm on $\mathbb{R}^n$, then for all $\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y} \in \mathbb{R}^n$,
$$
\|\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{y}\| \leq \|\mathbf{x}\| + \|\mathbf{y}\|.
$$
:::

::: {.definition #def-metric}
A **metric space** $(X, d)$ is a set $X$ together with a function $d: X \times X \to [0, \infty)$ satisfying: (i) $d(x,y) = 0 \iff x = y$; (ii) $d(x,y) = d(y,x)$; (iii) $d(x,z) \leq d(x,y) + d(y,z)$.
:::

::: {.proposition #prp-complete}
Every compact metric space is complete.
:::

::: {.remark}
The converse of @prp-complete is false: $\mathbb{R}$ with the standard metric is complete but not compact.
:::

By @thm-pythagoras and @thm-cauchy-schwarz, we can derive several important inequalities. See also @lem-triangle and @cor-norm for the triangle inequality in different settings. The notion of a metric space (@def-metric) unifies these ideas.

## Additional Results

::: {.theorem #thm-bolzano}
Every bounded sequence in $\mathbb{R}^n$ has a convergent subsequence.
:::

::: {.proof}
We prove the case $n = 1$; the general case follows by applying the one-dimensional result to each coordinate. Let $(x_k)$ be a bounded sequence in $\mathbb{R}$. By the "peak index" lemma, $(x_k)$ has a monotone subsequence. Since this subsequence is also bounded, it converges by the Monotone Convergence Theorem (see [-@thm-monotone]).
:::

::: {.theorem #thm-ivt}
If $f: [a,b] \to \mathbb{R}$ is continuous and $f(a) \cdot f(b) < 0$, then there exists $c \in (a,b)$ such that $f(c) = 0$.
:::

::: {.definition #def-continuity}
A function $f: X \to Y$ between metric spaces is **continuous** at $x_0 \in X$ if for every $\varepsilon > 0$ there exists $\delta > 0$ such that $d_X(x, x_0) < \delta$ implies $d_Y(f(x), f(x_0)) < \varepsilon$.
:::

::: {.example #exm-sqrt}
The function $f(x) = \sqrt{x}$ is continuous on $[0, \infty)$ but is not Lipschitz continuous at $x = 0$.
:::

## Topology Fundamentals

::: {.definition #def-topology}
A **topological space** is a pair $(X, \tau)$ where $X$ is a set and $\tau \subseteq \mathcal{P}(X)$ satisfying: (i) $\emptyset, X \in \tau$; (ii) $\tau$ is closed under arbitrary unions; (iii) $\tau$ is closed under finite intersections.
:::

::: {.definition #def-compact}
A topological space $X$ is **compact** if every open cover of $X$ has a finite subcover. That is, if $X \subseteq \bigcup_{\alpha \in A} U_\alpha$ with each $U_\alpha$ open, then there exist $\alpha_1, \ldots, \alpha_n \in A$ such that $X \subseteq U_{\alpha_1} \cup \cdots \cup U_{\alpha_n}$.
:::

::: {.theorem #thm-heine-borel}
A subset of $\mathbb{R}^n$ is compact if and only if it is closed and bounded.
:::

::: {.proof}
**Closed and bounded $\Rightarrow$ compact.** A closed and bounded subset $K \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n$ is contained in some closed box $[-M, M]^n$. By Tychonoff's theorem, $[-M, M]^n$ is compact. Since $K$ is a closed subset of a compact set, $K$ is compact.

**Compact $\Rightarrow$ closed and bounded.** Let $K$ be compact. Since $\mathbb{R}^n$ is Hausdorff, compact sets are closed. For boundedness, consider the open cover $\{B(0, k)\}_{k=1}^{\infty}$. By compactness, finitely many balls suffice, so $K \subseteq B(0, N)$ for some $N$.
:::

::: {.theorem #thm-tychonoff}
The product of any collection of compact topological spaces is compact in the product topology.
:::

::: {.lemma #lem-tube}
Let $X$ be a topological space, $Y$ a compact space, and $N$ an open set in $X \times Y$ containing $\{x_0\} \times Y$. Then there exists an open $W \subseteq X$ containing $x_0$ such that $W \times Y \subseteq N$.
:::

::: {.proposition #prp-hausdorff}
Every compact subspace of a Hausdorff space is closed.
:::

::: {.corollary #cor-compact-closed}
In a Hausdorff space, the intersection of a compact set and a closed set is compact.
:::


# Literature Review {#sec-litreview}

Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit[^4]. Praesent vestibulum molestie lacus. Aenean nonummy hendrerit mauris. Phasellus porta. Fusce suscipit varius mi. Cum sociis natoque penatibus et magnis dis parturient montes, nascetur ridiculus mus. Nulla dui. Fusce feugiat malesuada odio. Morbi nunc odio, gravida at, cursus nec, luctus a, lorem[^5].

Mauris fermentum dictum magna. Sed laoreet aliquam leo. Ut tellus dolor, dapibus eget, elementum vel, cursus eleifend, elit. Aenean auctor wisi et urna. Aliquam erat volutpat. Duis ac turpis.

As discussed in @sec-theory, the theoretical underpinnings are well established. The empirical evidence, however, remains mixed, as @arel2026quantitative have shown. We refer the reader to @thm-bolzano and @thm-ivt for the key convergence results. The foundational work of @rudin1976principles provides a rigorous treatment of the underlying analysis, while @neyman1933testing established the hypothesis testing framework we employ.

Integer rutrum ante eu lacus. Vestibulum libero nisl, porta vel, scelerisque eget, malesuada at, neque. Vivamus eget nibh. Etiam cursus leo vel metus. Nulla facilisi. Aenean nec eros. Nam elit magna, hendrerit sit amet, tincidunt ac, viverra sed, nulla. Donec pretium vulputate sapien.

The causal inference literature [@imbens2015causal] has grown considerably. Earlier work by @fisher1936use on experimental design laid the groundwork. @lehmann2005testing provides a comprehensive reference for testing methodology, while @pearson1901lines introduced the principal component analysis framework that underpins much modern multivariate work.

# Methods {#sec-methods}

## Data Description

Pellentesque habitant morbi tristique senectus et netus et malesuada fames ac turpis egestas. Donec odio elit, dictum in, hendrerit sit amet, egestas sed, leo. Praesent libero. Sed cursus ante dapibus diam. Sed nisi. Nulla quis sem at nibh elementum imperdiet. Duis sagittis ipsum.

::: {#tbl-vars}
| Variable | Type | Description | N |
|----------|------|-------------|---|
| `outcome` | Binary | Primary endpoint | 10,000 |
| `treatment` | Binary | Treatment assignment | 10,000 |
| `age` | Continuous | Age in years | 9,842 |
| `income` | Continuous | Annual income (USD) | 8,956 |
| `education` | Ordinal | Education level (1--5) | 9,991 |
| `region` | Categorical | Geographic region | 10,000 |
| `duration` | Continuous | Enrollment duration (months) | 10,000 |
| `baseline` | Continuous | Baseline score | 9,500 |

Summary of key variables in the analysis dataset.
:::

Praesent dapibus, neque id cursus faucibus, tortor neque egestas augue, eu vulputate magna eros eu erat. Aliquam erat volutpat. Nam dui mi, tincidunt quis, accumsan porttitor, facilisis luctus, metus.

::: {#tbl-balance}
| Statistic | Outcome=0 | Outcome=1 | Difference | p-value |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|------------|---------|
| Mean age | 42.3 | 39.1 | -3.2 | <0.001 |
| Mean income | \$52,400 | \$48,700 | -\$3,700 | 0.003 |
| % Female | 51.2% | 49.8% | -1.4pp | 0.312 |
| % Urban | 67.1% | 72.4% | +5.3pp | <0.001 |
| Mean baseline | 5.21 | 4.87 | -0.34 | 0.007 |
| Mean duration | 18.4 | 19.1 | +0.7 | 0.145 |

Balance table comparing covariates across outcome groups.
:::

::: {#tbl-regional}
| Region | N | % Treated | Mean Outcome | SE |
|--------|---|-----------|-------------|-----|
| Northeast | 2,450 | 50.2% | 0.342 | 0.010 |
| Southeast | 2,180 | 49.8% | 0.298 | 0.010 |
| Midwest | 2,370 | 50.5% | 0.331 | 0.010 |
| West | 1,890 | 49.3% | 0.356 | 0.011 |
| Southwest | 1,110 | 50.9% | 0.312 | 0.014 |

Regional distribution and summary statistics.
:::

## Statistical Models {#sec-models}

We consider three modeling approaches. The primary specification is a logistic regression:

$$
\log\left(\frac{p_i}{1-p_i}\right) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \text{treatment}_i + \mathbf{X}_i'\boldsymbol{\gamma} + \varepsilon_i
$$ {#eq-logit}

The secondary specification uses a probit link:

$$
\Phi^{-1}(p_i) = \alpha_0 + \alpha_1 \text{treatment}_i + \mathbf{X}_i'\boldsymbol{\delta}
$$ {#eq-probit}

And the third approach is a linear probability model:

$$
Y_i = \mu_0 + \mu_1 \text{treatment}_i + \mathbf{X}_i'\boldsymbol{\lambda} + u_i
$$ {#eq-lpm}

where $Y_i \in \{0, 1\}$ is the binary outcome and $\mathbf{X}_i$ is a vector of covariates including age, income, and education. The likelihood for the logistic model (@eq-logit) is

$$
\mathcal{L}(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \prod_{i=1}^n p_i^{y_i} (1 - p_i)^{1 - y_i}
$$

and the corresponding log-likelihood is

$$
\ell(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \sum_{i=1}^n \left[ y_i \log p_i + (1 - y_i) \log(1 - p_i) \right].
$$

The score equations are obtained by differentiating:

$$
\frac{\partial \ell}{\partial \boldsymbol{\theta}} = \sum_{i=1}^n (y_i - p_i) \mathbf{x}_i = \mathbf{0}.
$$

## Estimation Strategy

::: {.callout-note}
All models are estimated using maximum likelihood. Standard errors are clustered at the region level to account for within-region correlation.
:::

::: {.callout-warning}
The linear probability model can produce fitted probabilities outside the $[0, 1]$ interval. We report it for comparison but prefer the logistic specification.
:::

::: {.callout-tip title="Reproducibility"}
All code, data, and configuration files needed to reproduce these results are available in the project repository. See @sec-appendix-code for details.
:::

::: {.callout-note title="Nested Callout Example"}
This is the outer callout providing general context about our estimation approach.

::: {.callout-tip}
This nested callout contains a specific recommendation: always report both the logistic and LPM estimates as a robustness check.
:::

The outer context continues here with additional methodological notes.
:::


# Results {#sec-results}

## Main Findings

::: {.panel-tabset}

### Logistic Regression

The logistic regression yields an odds ratio of $\hat{\text{OR}} = 1.42$ (95% CI: 1.18--1.71, $p < 0.001$). This corresponds to a 42% increase in the odds of the outcome among treated individuals compared to controls.

Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit. Fusce a quam. Etiam ut purus mattis mauris sodales aliquam. Curabitur nisi. Quisque malesuada placerat nisl. Nam ipsum risus, rutrum vitae, vestibulum eu, molestie vel, lacus. Sed augue ipsum, egestas nec, vestibulum et, malesuada adipiscing, dui.

| Covariate | Estimate | SE | OR | 95% CI | p |
|-----------|----------|------|------|----------|-------|
| Treatment | 0.351 | 0.094 | 1.42 | 1.18--1.71 | <0.001 |
| Age | 0.023 | 0.005 | 1.02 | 1.01--1.03 | <0.001 |
| Income | 0.008 | 0.003 | 1.01 | 1.00--1.01 | 0.012 |
| Education | 0.142 | 0.041 | 1.15 | 1.06--1.25 | 0.001 |

### Probit Model

The probit model produces consistent results. The marginal effect of treatment at the mean is $\Delta p = 0.083$ (SE = 0.022), closely matching the average marginal effect from the logistic specification.

Vestibulum ante ipsum primis in faucibus orci luctus et ultrices posuere cubilia Curae; Morbi lacinia molestie dui. Praesent blandit dolor. Sed non quam. In vel mi sit amet augue congue elementum.

| Covariate | Estimate | SE | ME at mean | 95% CI | p |
|-----------|----------|------|------------|----------|-------|
| Treatment | 0.214 | 0.057 | 0.083 | 0.041--0.125 | <0.001 |
| Age | 0.014 | 0.003 | 0.005 | 0.002--0.009 | <0.001 |
| Income | 0.005 | 0.002 | 0.002 | 0.000--0.004 | 0.015 |
| Education | 0.087 | 0.025 | 0.034 | 0.015--0.052 | <0.001 |

### Linear Probability

The LPM estimates a treatment effect of $\hat{\beta}_1 = 0.079$ (SE = 0.021), which is comparable to the marginal effects from the nonlinear models. However, 3.2% of fitted values fall outside $[0, 1]$.

Sed dignissim lacinia nunc. Curabitur tortor. Pellentesque nibh. Aenean quam. In scelerisque sem at dolor. Maecenas mattis. Sed convallis tristique sem. Proin ut ligula vel nunc egestas porttitor.

| Covariate | Estimate | SE | 95% CI | p |
|-----------|----------|------|----------|-------|
| Treatment | 0.079 | 0.021 | 0.038--0.120 | <0.001 |
| Age | 0.005 | 0.001 | 0.003--0.008 | <0.001 |
| Income | 0.002 | 0.001 | 0.000--0.004 | 0.018 |
| Education | 0.032 | 0.009 | 0.014--0.050 | <0.001 |

:::


## Robustness Checks

::: {.panel-tabset}

### Subgroup Analysis

We examine treatment effects across demographic subgroups. The effect is largest among younger participants (age < 35) and those with lower baseline scores[^6].

Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit. Vivamus luctus urna sed urna ultricies ac tempor dui sagittis. In condimentum facilisis porta. Sed nec diam eu diam mattis viverra. Nulla fringilla, orci ac euismod semper, magna diam porttitor mauris, quis sollicitudin sapien justo in libero.

| Subgroup | N | OR | 95% CI | p-interaction |
|----------|---|------|----------|---------------|
| Age < 35 | 3,210 | 1.68 | 1.24--2.27 | 0.021 |
| Age 35--50 | 3,890 | 1.39 | 1.08--1.79 | --- |
| Age > 50 | 2,900 | 1.21 | 0.91--1.61 | --- |
| Low baseline | 4,750 | 1.55 | 1.22--1.97 | 0.038 |
| High baseline | 4,750 | 1.29 | 1.01--1.65 | --- |
| Female | 5,050 | 1.44 | 1.13--1.84 | 0.872 |
| Male | 4,950 | 1.40 | 1.09--1.80 | --- |

Vestibulum facilisis, purus nec pulvinar iaculis, ligula mi congue nunc, vitae euismod ligula urna in dolor. Mauris sollicitudin fermentum libero. Praesent nonummy mi in odio. Nunc interdum lacus sit amet orci. Vestibulum rutrum, mi nec elementum vehicula, eros quam gravida nisl, id fringilla neque ante vel mi.

### Sensitivity Analysis

Results are robust to alternative specifications including: (i) excluding observations with missing covariates, (ii) using inverse probability weighting, and (iii) controlling for pre-treatment trends[^7].

| Specification | OR | 95% CI | p | N |
|---------------|------|----------|-------|-------|
| Baseline | 1.42 | 1.18--1.71 | <0.001 | 10,000 |
| Complete cases | 1.45 | 1.19--1.77 | <0.001 | 8,214 |
| IPW | 1.40 | 1.15--1.70 | <0.001 | 10,000 |
| Pre-trends | 1.38 | 1.14--1.67 | 0.001 | 10,000 |
| Region FE | 1.43 | 1.19--1.72 | <0.001 | 10,000 |
| Propensity score | 1.41 | 1.16--1.71 | <0.001 | 9,800 |

Aliquam posuere. Nulla facilisis. Class aptent taciti sociosqu ad litora torquent per conubia nostra, per inceptos hymenaeos. Cras accumsan lorem in dui. Cum sociis natoque penatibus et magnis dis parturient montes, nascetur ridiculus mus. Donec quam felis, ultricies nec, pellentesque eu, pretium quis, sem.

Nulla consequat massa quis enim. Donec pede justo, fringilla vel, aliquet nec, vulputate eget, arcu. In enim justo, rhoncus ut, imperdiet a, venenatis vitae, justo. Nullam dictum felis eu pede mollis pretium. Integer tincidunt.

### Placebo Tests

We conduct placebo tests by randomly reassigning treatment status. Across 1,000 permutations, fewer than 5% produce treatment effects as large as our observed estimate, confirming that the result is unlikely due to chance[^8].

Cras dapibus. Vivamus elementum semper nisi. Aenean vulputate eleifend tellus. Aenean leo ligula, porttitor eu, consequat vitae, eleifend ac, enim. Aliquam lorem ante, dapibus in, viverra quis, feugiat a, tellus. Phasellus viverra nulla ut metus varius laoreet.

### Dose--Response

We examine whether treatment intensity modulates the effect by stratifying on exposure duration:

| Duration | N | OR | 95% CI | p |
|----------|---|------|----------|-------|
| < 6 months | 2,100 | 1.15 | 0.87--1.52 | 0.321 |
| 6--12 months | 3,400 | 1.38 | 1.08--1.76 | 0.009 |
| 12--24 months | 2,800 | 1.52 | 1.17--1.97 | 0.002 |
| > 24 months | 1,700 | 1.71 | 1.24--2.36 | 0.001 |

The monotonic increase in odds ratios with exposure duration supports a causal interpretation of the treatment effect. A formal test for trend yields $p_{\text{trend}} = 0.003$.

:::

[^6]: Sixth footnote --- Subgroup differences are statistically significant at the 5% level.
[^7]: Seventh footnote --- See online appendix for full sensitivity tables.
[^8]: Eighth footnote --- Permutation p-value = 0.032.


# Discussion {#sec-discussion}

The results in @sec-results confirm the theoretical predictions from @sec-theory. In particular, the treatment effect is both statistically significant and economically meaningful, with an odds ratio of 1.42 as established in the logistic regression.

Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit. Nam pretium turpis et arcu. Duis arcu tortor, suscipit eget, imperdiet nec, imperdiet iaculis, ipsum. Sed aliquam ultrices mauris. Integer ante arcu, accumsan a, consectetuer eget, posuere ut, mauris. Praesent adipiscing.

Phasellus ullamcorper ipsum rutrum nunc. Nunc nonummy metus. Vestibulum volutpat pretium libero. Cras id dui. Aenean ut eros et nisl sagittis vestibulum. Nullam nulla eros, ultricies sit amet, nonummy id, imperdiet feugiat, pede. Sed lectus. Donec mollis hendrerit risus. Phasellus nec sem in justo pellentesque facilisis[^9].

Etiam imperdiet imperdiet orci. Nunc nec neque. Phasellus leo dolor, tempus non, auctor et, hendrerit quis, nisi. Curabitur ligula sapien, tincidunt non, euismod vitae, posuere imperdiet, leo. Maecenas malesuada. Praesent congue erat at massa. Sed cursus turpis vitae tortor.

::: {.callout-important}
The effect size reported here is an intent-to-treat estimate. The local average treatment effect (LATE) for compliers is expected to be larger. Researchers should exercise caution when generalizing beyond the study population.
:::

::: {.callout-caution title="Limitations"}
Several limitations should be noted:

1. The sample is drawn from a single region and may not generalize nationally
2. Attrition was 12% over the study period, though attrition patterns are balanced across treatment and control
3. Self-reported outcomes may be subject to social desirability bias
4. The baseline period (2019--2021) overlaps with the COVID-19 pandemic, which may confound treatment effects
:::

The relationship between @thm-cauchy-schwarz and the empirical findings deserves further investigation. As @def-metric and @def-continuity formalize the relevant mathematical structures, future work should explore whether the continuity assumptions underlying our models are satisfied in practice. We note that @exm-sqrt provides a cautionary example.

[^9]: Ninth footnote --- The facilisis result follows from the general theory in @sec-theory.


# Extended Analysis {#sec-extended}

## Heterogeneous Effects

## Temporal Dynamics

Nunc feugiat mi a tellus consequat imperdiet. Vestibulum sapien. Proin quam. Etiam ultrices. Suspendisse in justo eu magna luctus suscipit. Sed lectus. Integer euismod lacus luctus magna[^11].

Quisque cursus, metus vitae pharetra auctor, sem massa mattis sem, at interdum magna augue eget diam. Vestibulum ante ipsum primis in faucibus orci luctus et ultrices posuere cubilia Curae; Morbi lacinia molestie dui. Praesent blandit dolor. Sed non quam. In vel mi sit amet augue congue elementum.

Morbi in ipsum sit amet pede facilisis laoreet. Donec lacus nunc, viverra nec, blandit vel, egestas et, augue. Vestibulum tincidunt malesuada tellus. Ut ultrices ultrices enim. Curabitur sit amet mauris. Morbi in dui quis est pulvinar ullamcorper. Nulla facilisi.

Integer lacinia sollicitudin massa. Cras metus. Sed aliquet risus a tortor. Integer id quam. Morbi mi. Quisque nisl felis, venenatis tristique, dignissim in, ultrices sit amet, augue. Proin sodales libero eget ante.

[^10]: Tenth footnote --- See supplementary materials for interaction plots.
[^11]: Eleventh footnote --- Temporal analysis uses quarterly aggregation.

## Mechanism Exploration

::: {.panel-tabset}

### Direct Effects

Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit. Ut purus elit, vestibulum ut, placerat ac, adipiscing vitae, felis. Curabitur dictum gravida mauris. Nam arcu libero, nonummy eget, consectetuer id, vulputate a, magna. Donec vehicula augue eu neque.

Pellentesque habitant morbi tristique senectus et netus et malesuada fames ac turpis egestas. Ut non enim eleifend felis pretium feugiat. Vivamus quis mi. Phasellus a est. Phasellus magna. In hac habitasse platea dictumst.

### Indirect Effects

Curabitur at lacus ac velit ornare lobortis. Curabitur a felis in nunc fringilla tristique. Morbi mattis ullamcorper velit. Phasellus gravida semper nisi. Nullam vel sem. Pellentesque libero tortor, tincidunt et, tincidunt eget, semper nec, quam.

Sed hendrerit. Maecenas nec odio et ante tincidunt tempus. Donec vitae sapien ut libero venenatis faucibus. Nullam quis ante. Etiam sit amet orci eget eros faucibus tincidunt. Duis leo. Sed fringilla mauris sit amet nibh.

### Mediation

Nam quam nunc, blandit vel, luctus pulvinar, hendrerit id, lorem. Maecenas nec odio et ante tincidunt tempus. Donec vitae sapien ut libero venenatis faucibus. Nullam quis ante. Etiam sit amet orci eget eros faucibus tincidunt.

In enim justo, rhoncus ut, imperdiet a, venenatis vitae, justo. Nullam dictum felis eu pede mollis pretium. Integer tincidunt. Cras dapibus. Vivamus elementum semper nisi. Aenean vulputate eleifend tellus.

### Spillover Effects

Aenean leo ligula, porttitor eu, consequat vitae, eleifend ac, enim. Aliquam lorem ante, dapibus in, viverra quis, feugiat a, tellus. Phasellus viverra nulla ut metus varius laoreet. Quisque rutrum. Aenean imperdiet.

Etiam ultricies nisi vel augue. Curabitur ullamcorper ultricies nisi. Nam eget dui. Etiam rhoncus. Maecenas tempus, tellus eget condimentum rhoncus, sem quam semper libero, sit amet adipiscing sem neque sed ipsum.

:::


# Mathematical Supplement {#sec-math}

## Key Inequalities

Building on @thm-cauchy-schwarz, we present additional results. The AM-GM inequality states:

$$
\frac{a_1 + a_2 + \cdots + a_n}{n} \geq \sqrt[n]{a_1 \cdot a_2 \cdots a_n}
$$

for non-negative reals $a_1, \ldots, a_n$. Jensen's inequality extends this:

$$
f\left(\sum_{i=1}^n \lambda_i x_i\right) \leq \sum_{i=1}^n \lambda_i f(x_i)
$$

for convex $f$ and weights $\lambda_i \geq 0$ with $\sum \lambda_i = 1$.

::: {.theorem #thm-holder}
For $p, q > 1$ with $\frac{1}{p} + \frac{1}{q} = 1$ and sequences $(a_i), (b_i) \geq 0$,
$$
\sum_{i=1}^n a_i b_i \leq \left(\sum_{i=1}^n a_i^p\right)^{1/p} \left(\sum_{i=1}^n b_i^q\right)^{1/q}.
$$
:::

::: {.proof}
By Young's inequality (@lem-young), for each $i$,
$$
\frac{a_i}{\|a\|_p} \cdot \frac{b_i}{\|b\|_q} \leq \frac{1}{p} \frac{a_i^p}{\|a\|_p^p} + \frac{1}{q} \frac{b_i^q}{\|b\|_q^q}.
$$
Summing over $i$ and using $\frac{1}{p} + \frac{1}{q} = 1$ yields the result.
:::

::: {.corollary #cor-cs-special}
Setting $p = q = 2$ in @thm-holder recovers @thm-cauchy-schwarz for finite-dimensional spaces.
:::

::: {.lemma #lem-young}
For $a, b \geq 0$ and $p, q > 1$ with $\frac{1}{p} + \frac{1}{q} = 1$,
$$
ab \leq \frac{a^p}{p} + \frac{b^q}{q}.
$$
:::

::: {.proof}
The function $f(t) = e^t$ is convex. By Jensen's inequality applied to the weights $\frac{1}{p}$ and $\frac{1}{q}$,
$$
e^{\frac{1}{p} \log a^p + \frac{1}{q} \log b^q} \leq \frac{1}{p} e^{\log a^p} + \frac{1}{q} e^{\log b^q} = \frac{a^p}{p} + \frac{b^q}{q}.
$$
The left-hand side simplifies to $e^{\log a + \log b} = ab$.
:::

::: {.conjecture #cnj-goldbach}
Every even integer greater than 2 can be expressed as the sum of two primes.
:::

::: {.conjecture #cnj-riemann}
All non-trivial zeros of the Riemann zeta function $\zeta(s) = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} n^{-s}$ have real part equal to $\frac{1}{2}$.
:::

## Convergence Theory

::: {.definition #def-convergence}
A sequence $(x_n)$ in a metric space $(X, d)$ **converges** to $x \in X$ if for every $\varepsilon > 0$ there exists $N \in \mathbb{N}$ such that $n \geq N$ implies $d(x_n, x) < \varepsilon$.
:::

::: {.theorem #thm-monotone}
Every bounded, monotone sequence of real numbers converges.
:::

::: {.proof}
Suppose $(x_n)$ is increasing and bounded above. Let $L = \sup_n x_n$, which exists by the completeness of $\mathbb{R}$. For any $\varepsilon > 0$, there exists $N$ with $x_N > L - \varepsilon$ (otherwise $L - \varepsilon$ would be an upper bound smaller than $L$). For $n \geq N$, $L - \varepsilon < x_N \leq x_n \leq L$, so $|x_n - L| < \varepsilon$. The decreasing case is analogous (take $\inf$).
:::

::: {.exercise #exr-bounded}
Show that convergent sequences in a metric space are bounded.
:::

::: {.solution}
Let $(x_n) \to x$. Choose $\varepsilon = 1$. Then there exists $N$ such that $d(x_n, x) < 1$ for all $n \geq N$. Set $M = \max\{d(x_1, x), \ldots, d(x_{N-1}, x), 1\}$. Then $d(x_n, x) \leq M$ for all $n$, so the sequence is bounded.
:::

::: {.exercise #exr-cauchy}
Prove that every convergent sequence in a metric space is a Cauchy sequence.
:::

::: {.solution}
Let $(x_n) \to x$ and let $\varepsilon > 0$. There exists $N$ such that $d(x_n, x) < \varepsilon/2$ for all $n \geq N$. For $m, n \geq N$,
$$
d(x_m, x_n) \leq d(x_m, x) + d(x, x_n) < \frac{\varepsilon}{2} + \frac{\varepsilon}{2} = \varepsilon.
$$
:::

The relationship between @thm-monotone and @thm-bolzano is fundamental: every bounded sequence has a monotone subsequence (by the "peak" argument), and every monotone bounded sequence converges. Together they yield Bolzano--Weierstrass.

## Measure Theory

::: {.definition #def-sigma-algebra}
A **$\sigma$-algebra** on a set $\Omega$ is a collection $\mathcal{F} \subseteq \mathcal{P}(\Omega)$ satisfying: (i) $\Omega \in \mathcal{F}$; (ii) if $A \in \mathcal{F}$ then $A^c \in \mathcal{F}$; (iii) if $A_1, A_2, \ldots \in \mathcal{F}$ then $\bigcup_{n=1}^{\infty} A_n \in \mathcal{F}$.
:::

::: {.definition #def-measure}
A **measure** on a measurable space $(\Omega, \mathcal{F})$ is a function $\mu: \mathcal{F} \to [0, \infty]$ satisfying: (i) $\mu(\emptyset) = 0$; (ii) countable additivity: for disjoint sets $A_1, A_2, \ldots \in \mathcal{F}$,
$$
\mu\left(\bigcup_{n=1}^{\infty} A_n\right) = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \mu(A_n).
$$
:::

::: {.theorem #thm-monotone-convergence}
Let $(f_n)$ be a sequence of non-negative measurable functions with $f_n \uparrow f$ pointwise. Then
$$
\lim_{n \to \infty} \int f_n \, d\mu = \int f \, d\mu.
$$
:::

::: {.theorem #thm-dominated-convergence}
Let $(f_n)$ be a sequence of measurable functions converging pointwise to $f$. If there exists an integrable function $g$ such that $|f_n| \leq g$ for all $n$, then
$$
\lim_{n \to \infty} \int f_n \, d\mu = \int f \, d\mu.
$$
:::

::: {.lemma #lem-fatou}
For non-negative measurable functions $(f_n)$,
$$
\int \liminf_{n \to \infty} f_n \, d\mu \leq \liminf_{n \to \infty} \int f_n \, d\mu.
$$
:::

::: {.proof}
Define $g_k = \inf_{n \geq k} f_n$. Then $g_k \leq f_n$ for all $n \geq k$, so $\int g_k \leq \int f_n$ for $n \geq k$, hence $\int g_k \leq \inf_{n \geq k} \int f_n$. Since $g_k \uparrow \liminf f_n$, the Monotone Convergence Theorem (@thm-monotone-convergence) gives
$$
\int \liminf f_n = \lim_k \int g_k \leq \lim_k \inf_{n \geq k} \int f_n = \liminf \int f_n.
$$
:::

## Linear Algebra

::: {.definition #def-eigenvalue}
Let $A$ be an $n \times n$ matrix. A scalar $\lambda$ is an **eigenvalue** of $A$ if there exists a nonzero vector $\mathbf{v}$ such that $A\mathbf{v} = \lambda \mathbf{v}$. The vector $\mathbf{v}$ is the corresponding **eigenvector**.
:::

::: {.theorem #thm-spectral}
Every real symmetric matrix $A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ has a decomposition $A = Q \Lambda Q^T$ where $Q$ is orthogonal and $\Lambda$ is diagonal with real eigenvalues.
:::

::: {.proof}
We proceed by induction on $n$. The base case $n = 1$ is trivial. For the inductive step, let $\lambda_1$ be an eigenvalue of $A$ (which exists since $\det(A - \lambda I)$ has a real root for real symmetric $A$) with unit eigenvector $\mathbf{v}_1$. Extend $\mathbf{v}_1$ to an orthonormal basis $\{\mathbf{v}_1, \mathbf{v}_2, \ldots, \mathbf{v}_n\}$ and set $P = [\mathbf{v}_1 \mid \mathbf{v}_2 \mid \cdots \mid \mathbf{v}_n]$. Then

$$
P^T A P = \begin{pmatrix} \lambda_1 & \mathbf{0}^T \\ \mathbf{0} & B \end{pmatrix}
$$

where $B$ is a real symmetric $(n-1) \times (n-1)$ matrix. By the inductive hypothesis, $B = Q' \Lambda' Q'^T$. Assembling gives the result.
:::

::: {.corollary #cor-positive-definite}
A real symmetric matrix $A$ is positive definite if and only if all its eigenvalues are strictly positive.
:::

::: {#tbl-definiteness}
| Property | Positive Definite | Positive Semidefinite | Indefinite |
|----------|-------------------|----------------------|------------|
| Eigenvalues | All $> 0$ | All $\geq 0$ | Mixed signs |
| Determinant | $> 0$ | $\geq 0$ | Any |
| Leading minors | All $> 0$ | --- | --- |
| Quadratic form | $\mathbf{x}^T A \mathbf{x} > 0$ | $\mathbf{x}^T A \mathbf{x} \geq 0$ | Both signs |
| Cholesky | Exists | Exists (with zeros) | Does not exist |

Classification of symmetric matrices by definiteness.
:::


# Probability and Statistics {#sec-probability}

## Foundational Results

::: {.theorem #thm-lln}
**(Strong Law of Large Numbers.)** Let $X_1, X_2, \ldots$ be i.i.d. random variables with $\mathbb{E}[|X_1|] < \infty$. Then
$$
\frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^n X_i \xrightarrow{\text{a.s.}} \mathbb{E}[X_1].
$$
:::

::: {.theorem #thm-clt}
**(Central Limit Theorem.)** Let $X_1, X_2, \ldots$ be i.i.d. with $\mathbb{E}[X_1] = \mu$ and $\text{Var}(X_1) = \sigma^2 < \infty$. Then
$$
\sqrt{n}\left(\bar{X}_n - \mu\right) \xrightarrow{d} N(0, \sigma^2).
$$
:::

::: {.theorem #thm-delta-method}
**(Delta Method.)** If $\sqrt{n}(T_n - \theta) \xrightarrow{d} N(0, \sigma^2)$ and $g$ is differentiable at $\theta$ with $g'(\theta) \neq 0$, then
$$
\sqrt{n}\left(g(T_n) - g(\theta)\right) \xrightarrow{d} N\left(0, [g'(\theta)]^2 \sigma^2\right).
$$
:::

::: {.example #exm-delta}
To find the asymptotic distribution of $\log \bar{X}_n$ when $X_i \sim \text{Exponential}(\lambda)$: we have $\mu = 1/\lambda$, $\sigma^2 = 1/\lambda^2$, and $g(x) = \log x$. By @thm-delta-method,
$$
\sqrt{n}\left(\log \bar{X}_n - \log \frac{1}{\lambda}\right) \xrightarrow{d} N\left(0, \lambda^2 \cdot \frac{1}{\lambda^2}\right) = N(0, 1).
$$
:::

## Bayesian Inference

::: {.theorem #thm-bayes}
**(Bayes' Theorem.)** For events $A$ and $B$ with $P(B) > 0$,
$$
P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A) \, P(A)}{P(B)}.
$$
:::

The posterior distribution for a parameter $\theta$ given data $\mathbf{y}$ is

$$
\pi(\theta \mid \mathbf{y}) = \frac{L(\mathbf{y} \mid \theta) \, \pi(\theta)}{\int L(\mathbf{y} \mid \theta') \, \pi(\theta') \, d\theta'}
$$

where $L(\mathbf{y} \mid \theta)$ is the likelihood and $\pi(\theta)$ is the prior. Conjugate priors yield closed-form posteriors:

::: {#tbl-conjugate}
| Likelihood | Conjugate Prior | Posterior |
|-----------|----------------|-----------|
| Normal($\mu$, $\sigma^2$ known) | Normal($\mu_0$, $\tau^2$) | Normal($\tilde{\mu}$, $\tilde{\tau}^2$) |
| Binomial($n$, $p$) | Beta($\alpha$, $\beta$) | Beta($\alpha + x$, $\beta + n - x$) |
| Poisson($\lambda$) | Gamma($\alpha$, $\beta$) | Gamma($\alpha + \sum x_i$, $\beta + n$) |
| Exponential($\lambda$) | Gamma($\alpha$, $\beta$) | Gamma($\alpha + n$, $\beta + \sum x_i$) |

Common conjugate prior families.
:::

::: {.callout-tip title="Jeffreys' Prior"}
When no prior information is available, Jeffreys' prior $\pi(\theta) \propto \sqrt{I(\theta)}$, where $I(\theta)$ is the Fisher information, provides a non-informative prior that is invariant under reparameterization.
:::


# Conditional Content {#sec-conditional}

The following content demonstrates format-specific rendering. Different output formats will display different material.

::: {.content-visible when-format="html"}
This paragraph is **only visible in HTML output**. It contains an HTML-specific note about interactive features and JavaScript rendering.
:::

::: {.content-visible when-format="latex"}
This paragraph is **only visible in LaTeX output**. It contains a note about PDF compilation and page layout.
:::

::: {.content-visible when-format="typst"}
This paragraph is **only visible in Typst output**. Typst is a modern typesetting system.
:::

::: {.content-hidden when-format="html"}
This paragraph is **hidden from HTML** but visible in all other formats.
:::

::: {.content-visible when-format="markdown"}
This paragraph is **only visible in Markdown output**. It is stripped from HTML, LaTeX, and Typst.
:::


# Raw Format Blocks {#sec-raw-blocks}

The following sections contain format-specific raw content:

```{=html}
<div style="border: 2px solid #3498db; padding: 1em; border-radius: 8px; margin: 1em 0;">
  <strong>HTML-only box:</strong> This styled <code>&lt;div&gt;</code> only appears in HTML output.
  It is passed through verbatim without markdown processing.
</div>
```

```{=latex}
\begin{center}
\fbox{\parbox{0.8\textwidth}{%
\textbf{LaTeX-only box:} This framed paragraph only appears in LaTeX/PDF output.
It uses native LaTeX commands.}}
\end{center}
```

```{=typst}
#rect(
  width: 100%,
  fill: luma(240),
  inset: 1em,
  [*Typst-only box:* This rectangle only appears in Typst output.]
)
```


# Spans and Variables {#sec-shortcodes}

This document's title is "{{ meta.title }}" by {{ meta.author }}, dated {{ meta.date }}.

The abstract begins: "{{ meta.abstract }}"

[]{.pagebreak}


# Advanced Mathematics {#sec-advanced-math}

## Differential Equations

The heat equation in one dimension is

$$
\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = \kappa \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}
$$ {#eq-heat}

with boundary conditions $u(0, t) = u(L, t) = 0$ and initial condition $u(x, 0) = f(x)$. The solution via separation of variables is

$$
u(x, t) = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} B_n \sin\left(\frac{n\pi x}{L}\right) e^{-\kappa (n\pi/L)^2 t}
$$

where the Fourier coefficients are

$$
B_n = \frac{2}{L} \int_0^L f(x) \sin\left(\frac{n\pi x}{L}\right) dx.
$$

The wave equation,

$$
\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial t^2} = c^2 \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2},
$$ {#eq-wave}

and the Laplace equation,

$$
\nabla^2 \phi = \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial y^2} = 0,
$$ {#eq-laplace}

are the other fundamental PDEs of mathematical physics. Together, @eq-heat, @eq-wave, and @eq-laplace form the classical trio.

## Matrix Calculus

For a matrix $A \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ and vectors $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$, $\mathbf{b} \in \mathbb{R}^m$:

$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial \mathbf{x}} \| A\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b} \|^2 = 2 A^T(A\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b})
$$

Setting this to zero gives the normal equations:

$$
A^T A \hat{\mathbf{x}} = A^T \mathbf{b}
$$

The solution $\hat{\mathbf{x}} = (A^T A)^{-1} A^T \mathbf{b}$ is the ordinary least squares estimator. Its covariance matrix is

$$
\text{Cov}(\hat{\mathbf{x}}) = \sigma^2 (A^T A)^{-1}
$$

and the hat matrix $H = A(A^T A)^{-1} A^T$ projects onto the column space of $A$.

## Functional Analysis

::: {.definition #def-banach}
A **Banach space** is a complete normed vector space --- that is, a normed space $(V, \|\cdot\|)$ in which every Cauchy sequence converges.
:::

::: {.definition #def-hilbert}
A **Hilbert space** is a Banach space whose norm arises from an inner product: $\|x\| = \sqrt{\langle x, x \rangle}$.
:::

::: {.theorem #thm-riesz}
**(Riesz Representation Theorem.)** Let $H$ be a Hilbert space and $\varphi: H \to \mathbb{R}$ a continuous linear functional. Then there exists a unique $y \in H$ such that $\varphi(x) = \langle x, y \rangle$ for all $x \in H$.
:::

::: {.theorem #thm-banach-fixed}
**(Banach Fixed-Point Theorem.)** Let $(X, d)$ be a complete metric space and $T: X \to X$ a contraction mapping with constant $q < 1$. Then $T$ has a unique fixed point $x^* \in X$, and for any $x_0 \in X$, $T^n(x_0) \to x^*$.
:::

::: {.proof}
**Existence.** Pick any $x_0$ and define $x_n = T(x_{n-1})$. For $m > n$,
$$
d(x_n, x_m) \leq \sum_{k=n}^{m-1} d(x_k, x_{k+1}) \leq \sum_{k=n}^{m-1} q^k d(x_0, x_1) \leq \frac{q^n}{1-q} d(x_0, x_1).
$$
Since $q < 1$, the right side $\to 0$ as $n \to \infty$, so $(x_n)$ is Cauchy. By completeness, $x_n \to x^*$ for some $x^*$. Since $T$ is continuous, $T(x^*) = \lim T(x_n) = \lim x_{n+1} = x^*$.

**Uniqueness.** If $T(y^*) = y^*$ also, then $d(x^*, y^*) = d(T(x^*), T(y^*)) \leq q \cdot d(x^*, y^*)$. Since $q < 1$, this forces $d(x^*, y^*) = 0$.
:::

::: {.algorithm #alg-picard}
**(Picard iteration for ODEs.)**
Given an initial value problem $y' = f(t, y)$, $y(t_0) = y_0$:

1. Set $\phi_0(t) = y_0$
2. For $n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$, compute $\phi_{n+1}(t) = y_0 + \int_{t_0}^t f(s, \phi_n(s)) \, ds$
3. If $f$ is Lipschitz in $y$, the sequence $(\phi_n)$ converges uniformly to the unique solution
:::


# Bulk Content for Stress Testing {#sec-bulk}

## Prose Section Alpha

Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit. Donec vel sapien velit. Fusce id eros at diam maximus gravida sit amet in tellus. Suspendisse potenti. Praesent sodales ligula quis erat tempor, sit amet fermentum justo lobortis. Nulla id lacinia justo. Proin a felis vel ligula tristique porttitor. Aliquam suscipit semper tellus, ut dapibus mi efficitur nec^[Inline footnote demonstrating this syntax.]. Sed eu velit velit. Nullam scelerisque volutpat dui quis rhoncus. Integer vel elit vel lacus vehicula lobortis a ut mauris.

Vivamus quis purus at augue ultricies accumsan eget sit amet velit. Quisque tincidunt, quam eget tempus aliquam, ligula velit dictum purus, et vehicula odio velit at arcu. Nullam ultrices mi at turpis fermentum, non vehicula mi eleifend. Proin et orci id nisl ultricies placerat. Cras nec turpis vel metus aliquam eleifend a in lectus. Donec sodales feugiat ipsum, at blandit diam ultrices sit amet. Pellentesque sed ex ut augue volutpat imperdiet.

Etiam sit amet eros a metus varius euismod eget non eros. Sed consequat augue vel libero feugiat, in fringilla sapien aliquam. Sed auctor lacus in urna fermentum, a molestie arcu pellentesque. Ut posuere risus ut ex condimentum, nec cursus lorem vestibulum. Morbi vestibulum mauris ut lacus fermentum, quis feugiat nunc efficitur. Fusce nec est quis est feugiat tempor vel eu velit. Curabitur volutpat purus at libero tincidunt, non suscipit erat varius.

## Prose Section Beta

Phasellus imperdiet, nulla et dictum interdum, nisi lorem egestas odio, vitae scelerisque enim ligula venenatis dolor. Maecenas nisl est, ultrices nec congue eget, auctor vitae massa. Fusce luctus vestibulum augue ut facilisis. Cras imperdiet nunc nec purus euismod, id euismod elit maximus. Nunc lobortis dolor quis mi varius, in bibendum nibh facilisis. Sed convallis erat id turpis posuere lobortis. Sed eu ligula risus.

Nullam mollis sem in urna tincidunt, sit amet venenatis augue tempor. Mauris pulvinar ex in turpis lacinia, a dignissim leo pellentesque. Nunc non tempor sapien. Donec vel elit pretium, placerat ante nec, blandit nulla. Integer ut turpis varius, commodo ex in, facilisis nunc. Aliquam erat volutpat. Fusce euismod lobortis lacus vel tempus. Quisque congue nunc eget erat tincidunt facilisis. Cras sit amet dolor sit amet turpis bibendum dignissim.

Ut vehicula diam eget nunc blandit, in tempor orci luctus. Nulla facilisi. Pellentesque habitant morbi tristique senectus et netus et malesuada fames ac turpis egestas. Cras hendrerit nisl vel lacinia fermentum. Donec nec sapien vel ligula tincidunt eleifend a non arcu. Morbi et ex sed massa interdum convallis id ac lorem. Vestibulum vitae velit et purus vestibulum pharetra sit amet vel magna.

## Prose Section Gamma

Aliquam nec felis eu purus suscipit tincidunt sit amet a enim. Donec euismod dolor quis est tempus facilisis. Morbi non ante quam. Nullam lobortis erat id augue lacinia, id feugiat mi lobortis. Phasellus vel quam ac libero suscipit commodo. Aenean sagittis diam vel eros tincidunt, a pharetra ligula interdum. Praesent lacinia sapien sit amet consequat ultricies.

Fusce fermentum est ut quam ultrices, id posuere risus accumsan. Proin hendrerit lorem sed orci vestibulum, vel iaculis purus posuere. Ut sit amet velit sed mauris malesuada auctor non a nibh. Nullam vel velit eget sapien aliquam maximus ut nec lorem. Curabitur lobortis erat vel enim accumsan, quis sagittis nulla sollicitudin. In lacinia turpis eu ante maximus, ac posuere ex lacinia.

Vivamus sit amet dolor vitae mauris sagittis fringilla. Praesent eget lacus vel justo fermentum facilisis. Cras a velit vel dolor condimentum tempor. Morbi sodales ante a sem suscipit, id euismod sapien consequat. Sed aliquet lacus eu sapien posuere, vel accumsan dui blandit. Nulla in velit in risus finibus bibendum sed nec est.

Integer nec turpis a dui cursus aliquam eget eget diam. Proin consectetur vestibulum sapien at convallis. Nullam tempus mauris non lectus efficitur hendrerit. Vivamus semper augue nec justo euismod, eu varius dolor sodales. Cras fringilla neque at felis pretium, et pretium est interdum. Mauris molestie sapien sit amet mi maximus, eu dapibus felis dictum.

## Prose Section Delta

### Subsection Delta-One

Pellentesque dapibus suscipit ligula. Donec posuere augue in quam. Etiam vel tortor sodales tellus ultricies commodo. Suspendisse potenti. Aenean in sem ac leo mollis blandit. Donec neque quam, dignissim in, mollis nec, sagittis eu, wisi. Phasellus lacus. Etiam laoreet quam sed arcu.

#### Sub-subsection Delta-One-Alpha

Phasellus at dui in ligula mollis ultricies. Integer placerat tristique nisl. Praesent augue. Fusce commodo. Vestibulum convallis, lorem a tempus semper, dui dui euismod elit, vitae placerat urna tortor vitae lacus. Nullam libero mauris, consequat quis, varius et, dictum id, arcu.

#### Sub-subsection Delta-One-Beta

Donec vitae arcu. Nam pretium turpis et arcu. Duis arcu tortor, suscipit eget, imperdiet nec, imperdiet iaculis, ipsum. Sed aliquam ultrices mauris. Integer ante arcu, accumsan a, consectetuer eget, posuere ut, mauris.

### Subsection Delta-Two

Maecenas vestibulum mollis diam. Pellentesque ut neque. Pellentesque habitant morbi tristique senectus et netus et malesuada fames ac turpis egestas. In dui magna, posuere eget, vestibulum et, tempor auctor, justo. In ac felis quis tortor malesuada pretium.

#### Sub-subsection Delta-Two-Alpha

Pellentesque auctor neque nec urna. Proin sapien ipsum, porta a, auctor quis, eu amet, nibh. Cras id dui. Aenean ut eros et nisl sagittis vestibulum. Nullam nulla eros, ultricies sit amet, nonummy id, imperdiet feugiat, pede. Vivamus vel sapien.

Praesent ac massa at ligula laoreet iaculis. Nulla neque dolor, sagittis eget, iaculis quis, molestie non, velit. Mauris turpis nunc, blandit et, volutpat molestie, porta ut, ligula.

#### Sub-subsection Delta-Two-Beta

Fusce ac felis sit amet ligula pharetra condimentum. Maecenas egestas arcu quis ligula mattis placerat. Duis lobortis massa imperdiet quam. Suspendisse potenti. Pellentesque commodo eros a enim.

### Subsection Delta-Three

Vestibulum ante ipsum primis in faucibus orci luctus et ultrices posuere cubilia Curae; In ac dui quis mi consectetuer lacinia. Nam pretium turpis et arcu. Duis arcu tortor, suscipit eget, imperdiet nec, imperdiet iaculis, ipsum.

Sed aliquam ultrices mauris. Integer ante arcu, accumsan a, consectetuer eget, posuere ut, mauris. Praesent adipiscing. Phasellus ullamcorper ipsum rutrum nunc. Nunc nonummy metus. Vestibulum volutpat pretium libero.

## Prose Section Epsilon

### Subsection Epsilon-One

Cras varius. Donec vitae orci sed dolor rutrum auctor. Fusce egestas elit eget lorem. Suspendisse nisl elit, rhoncus eget, elementum ac, condimentum eget, diam. Nam at tortor in tellus interdum sagittis. Aliquam lobortis.

Donec orci lectus, aliquam vel, dapibus a, posuere nec, elit. Nullam ante orci, pellentesque eget, tempus quis, ultrices in, est. Curabitur sit amet nulla. Nam in massa.

### Subsection Epsilon-Two

Vivamus luctus egestas leo. Maecenas sollicitudin. Nullam rhoncus aliquam metus. Etiam egestas wisi a erat. Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetuer adipiscing elit. Morbi vel erat non mauris convallis vehicula^[Another inline footnote for stress testing.]. Nulla et sapien.

Integer tortor tellus, aliquam faucibus, convallis id, congue eu, quam. Mauris ullamcorper felis vitae erat. Proin feugiat, augue non elementum posuere, metus purus iaculis lectus, et tristique ligula justo vitae magna.

Aliquam convallis sollicitudin purus. Praesent aliquam, enim at fermentum mollis, ligula massa adipiscing nisl, ac euismod nibh nisl eu lectus. Fusce vulputate sem at sapien. Vivamus leo. Aliquam euismod libero eu enim. Nulla nec felis sed leo placerat imperdiet. Aenean suscipit nulla in justo. Suspendisse cursus rutrum augue.

### Subsection Epsilon-Three

#### Deep Nesting Level Four

Nulla quam. Aenean laoreet. Vestibulum nisi lectus, commodo ac, facilisis ac, ultricies eu, pede. Ut orci risus, accumsan porttitor, cursus quis, aliquet eget, justo.

##### Even Deeper Level Five

Sed pretium blandit orci. Ut eu diam at pede suscipit sodales. Aenean lectus elit, fermentum non, convallis id, sagittis at, neque. Nullam mauris orci, aliquet et, iaculis et, viverra vitae, ligula.

Nunc nec lectus in pede hendrerit laoreet. Nulla quam. Aenean laoreet. Vestibulum nisi lectus, commodo ac, facilisis ac, ultricies eu, pede.

## Dense Mathematics

Consider the multivariate normal distribution $\mathbf{X} \sim N(\boldsymbol{\mu}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma})$. The density is

$$
f(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{n/2} |\boldsymbol{\Sigma}|^{1/2}} \exp\left(-\frac{1}{2} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})\right).
$$

The moment generating function is $M(\mathbf{t}) = \exp\left(\boldsymbol{\mu}^T \mathbf{t} + \frac{1}{2} \mathbf{t}^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{t}\right)$. The characteristic function is $\varphi(\mathbf{t}) = \exp\left(i \boldsymbol{\mu}^T \mathbf{t} - \frac{1}{2} \mathbf{t}^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{t}\right)$.

Key properties: if $\mathbf{X} \sim N(\boldsymbol{\mu}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma})$ and $\mathbf{Y} = A\mathbf{X} + \mathbf{b}$, then $\mathbf{Y} \sim N(A\boldsymbol{\mu} + \mathbf{b}, A \boldsymbol{\Sigma} A^T)$. For a partition

$$
\mathbf{X} = \begin{pmatrix} \mathbf{X}_1 \\ \mathbf{X}_2 \end{pmatrix}, \quad
\boldsymbol{\mu} = \begin{pmatrix} \boldsymbol{\mu}_1 \\ \boldsymbol{\mu}_2 \end{pmatrix}, \quad
\boldsymbol{\Sigma} = \begin{pmatrix} \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{11} & \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{12} \\ \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{21} & \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{22} \end{pmatrix},
$$

the conditional distribution is $\mathbf{X}_1 \mid \mathbf{X}_2 = \mathbf{x}_2 \sim N(\bar{\boldsymbol{\mu}}, \bar{\boldsymbol{\Sigma}})$ where

$$
\bar{\boldsymbol{\mu}} = \boldsymbol{\mu}_1 + \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{12} \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{22}^{-1} (\mathbf{x}_2 - \boldsymbol{\mu}_2), \quad
\bar{\boldsymbol{\Sigma}} = \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{11} - \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{12} \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{22}^{-1} \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_{21}.
$$

The Mahalanobis distance is $D^2 = (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})^T \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} (\mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})$, which follows a $\chi^2_n$ distribution when $\mathbf{X} \sim N(\boldsymbol{\mu}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma})$.

## More Tables

::: {#tbl-distributions}
| Distribution | PDF/PMF | Mean | Variance | MGF |
|-------------|---------|------|----------|-----|
| $\text{Bernoulli}(p)$ | $p^x(1-p)^{1-x}$ | $p$ | $p(1-p)$ | $(1-p) + pe^t$ |
| $\text{Binomial}(n,p)$ | $\binom{n}{x}p^x(1-p)^{n-x}$ | $np$ | $np(1-p)$ | $((1-p)+pe^t)^n$ |
| $\text{Poisson}(\lambda)$ | $e^{-\lambda}\lambda^x/x!$ | $\lambda$ | $\lambda$ | $e^{\lambda(e^t-1)}$ |
| $\text{Geometric}(p)$ | $(1-p)^{x-1}p$ | $1/p$ | $(1-p)/p^2$ | $pe^t/(1-(1-p)e^t)$ |
| $N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ | $(2\pi\sigma^2)^{-1/2}e^{-(x-\mu)^2/2\sigma^2}$ | $\mu$ | $\sigma^2$ | $e^{\mu t + \sigma^2 t^2/2}$ |
| $\text{Exp}(\lambda)$ | $\lambda e^{-\lambda x}$ | $1/\lambda$ | $1/\lambda^2$ | $\lambda/(\lambda-t)$ |
| $\text{Gamma}(\alpha,\beta)$ | $\frac{\beta^\alpha}{\Gamma(\alpha)}x^{\alpha-1}e^{-\beta x}$ | $\alpha/\beta$ | $\alpha/\beta^2$ | $(\beta/(\beta-t))^\alpha$ |
| $\text{Beta}(\alpha,\beta)$ | $\frac{x^{\alpha-1}(1-x)^{\beta-1}}{B(\alpha,\beta)}$ | $\frac{\alpha}{\alpha+\beta}$ | $\frac{\alpha\beta}{(\alpha+\beta)^2(\alpha+\beta+1)}$ | --- |

Common probability distributions and their properties.
:::

::: {#tbl-tests}
| Test | Null Hypothesis | Test Statistic | Distribution under $H_0$ |
|------|----------------|----------------|--------------------------|
| $z$-test | $\mu = \mu_0$ | $Z = \frac{\bar{X} - \mu_0}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}}$ | $N(0,1)$ |
| $t$-test | $\mu = \mu_0$ | $T = \frac{\bar{X} - \mu_0}{S/\sqrt{n}}$ | $t_{n-1}$ |
| $\chi^2$ goodness-of-fit | $p_i = p_{i0}$ | $\chi^2 = \sum \frac{(O_i - E_i)^2}{E_i}$ | $\chi^2_{k-1}$ |
| $F$-test | $\sigma_1^2 = \sigma_2^2$ | $F = S_1^2 / S_2^2$ | $F_{n_1-1, n_2-1}$ |
| Wald test | $\theta = \theta_0$ | $W = (\hat\theta - \theta_0)^2 / \widehat{\text{Var}}(\hat\theta)$ | $\chi^2_1$ |
| LR test | $\theta \in \Theta_0$ | $\Lambda = -2\log(L_0/L_1)$ | $\chi^2_r$ |

Common hypothesis tests in statistics.
:::


# Conclusion {#sec-conclusion}

This document has demonstrated the interplay between @sec-theory and @sec-results. The treatment effect, estimated at OR = 1.42 via logistic regression, is robust across specifications (@sec-results). The mathematical foundations from @sec-math --- particularly @thm-holder and @lem-young --- provide the analytical scaffolding for asymptotic inference.

As @arel2026quantitative emphasize, adequate statistical power remains a pressing concern. Our sample of $n = 10{,}000$ provides 92% power to detect effects of the magnitude observed, well above conventional thresholds [-@arel2026quantitative]. The classical results of @rudin1976principles and @lehmann2005testing provide the theoretical grounding, while @knuth1997art reminds us of the importance of algorithmic efficiency in computation.

The topology results from @sec-theory --- @thm-heine-borel, @thm-tychonoff, and @prp-hausdorff --- are central to modern analysis. The measure-theoretic results in @sec-math --- @thm-monotone-convergence, @thm-dominated-convergence, and @lem-fatou --- underpin the probabilistic results of @sec-probability including @thm-lln and @thm-clt.

Future research should address the limitations discussed in @sec-discussion and extend the temporal analysis from @sec-extended. In particular, the mechanisms identified in the mediation analysis warrant further investigation using experimental designs with stronger identification strategies[^12]. The Banach fixed-point theorem (@thm-banach-fixed) and the Riesz representation theorem (@thm-riesz) suggest fruitful connections to functional-analytic methods in econometrics.

The model specifications @eq-logit, @eq-probit, and @eq-lpm cover the standard binary-choice toolbox. The balance table (@tbl-balance) confirms adequate randomization, while @tbl-regional reveals geographic heterogeneity. The data summary in @tbl-vars lists all covariates. Distributional properties are collected in @tbl-distributions, hypothesis tests in @tbl-tests, and matrix definiteness criteria in @tbl-definiteness.

[^12]: Twelfth footnote --- Forthcoming experimental protocols are registered at osf.io/example.


# Appendix: Code {#sec-appendix-code}

## `R`

```r
# Logistic regression specification
model_logit <- glm(
  outcome ~ treatment + age + income + education,
  data = df,
  family = binomial(link = "logit")
)

# Probit specification
model_probit <- glm(
  outcome ~ treatment + age + income + education,
  data = df,
  family = binomial(link = "probit")
)

# Linear probability model
model_lpm <- lm(
  outcome ~ treatment + age + income + education,
  data = df
)

# Clustered standard errors
library(sandwich)
library(lmtest)
coeftest(model_logit, vcov = vcovCL(model_logit, cluster = df$region))
```

## `Python`

```python
import statsmodels.api as sm
import numpy as np

# Logistic regression
X = df[["treatment", "age", "income", "education"]]
X = sm.add_constant(X)
y = df["outcome"]

model = sm.Logit(y, X).fit(cov_type="cluster", cov_kwds={"groups": df["region"]})
print(model.summary())

# Marginal effects
mfx = model.get_margeff()
print(mfx.summary())
```

## `Rust`

```rust
use nalgebra::{DMatrix, DVector};

fn ols(x: &DMatrix<f64>, y: &DVector<f64>) -> DVector<f64> {
    let xt = x.transpose();
    let xtx = &xt * x;
    let xty = &xt * y;
    xtx.lu().solve(&xty).expect("Singular matrix")
}

fn residuals(x: &DMatrix<f64>, y: &DVector<f64>, beta: &DVector<f64>) -> DVector<f64> {
    y - x * beta
}
```

## `Julia`

```julia
using GLM, DataFrames

# Logistic regression
model = glm(@formula(outcome ~ treatment + age + income + education),
            df, Binomial(), LogitLink())
coeftable(model)
```

> All analyses were conducted in R 4.4.2, Python 3.12, Rust 1.89, and Julia 1.11. Results were rendered with *Calepin*.

::: {.callout-note title="Data Availability" collapse="true"}
The dataset used in this analysis is available upon request from the corresponding author. A synthetic version of the data, suitable for replication of the statistical methods, is included in the repository under `data/synthetic.csv`.

Fusce mauris. Vestibulum luctus nibh at lectus. Sed bibendum, nulla a faucibus semper, leo velit ultricies tellus, ac venenatis arcu wisi vel nisl. Vestibulum diam. Aliquam pellentesque, augue quis sagittis posuere, turpis lacus congue quam, in hendrerit risus eros eget felis.
:::
